planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost; or divide established clumps in early spring. Division every 2-3 years keeps plants vigorous and prevents the center die-out typical of older clumps.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile to average, well-drained soil in full sun to very light shade; New York aster is among the most profusely blooming of the native fall asters, producing dense masses of violet-blue to purple or white daisy flowers from late August through October and serving as one of the most important late-season nectar sources for migrating monarchs, painted ladies, and bumble bee queens preparing for winter. Divide clumps every two to three years in early spring to maintain vigor and prevent the hollow center that develops in older undivided plants. Many cultivars offer compact habits, deeper colors, and extended bloom periods. Susceptible to powdery mildew in hot, humid climates; choose resistant varieties and provide good air circulation.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to dry shaded soil; this aster spreads by both seed and rhizome to form dense groundcover colonies. It tolerates poor, dry, acidic conditions under oaks and maples better than almost any other flowering perennial.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, acidic, humus-rich to poor soil in partial to full shade; large-leaf wood aster is one of the most shade and drought-tolerant native flowering plants in the northeastern US, forming low-growing groundcover colonies beneath dense oak canopy where few other flowering plants survive. The huge, heart-shaped basal leaves are distinctive and nearly a foot across. Pale lavender to white daisy flowers appear in late summer and fall, providing valuable late-season nectar under closed canopy where most other pollinators run out of food. It spreads vigorously by rhizome in favorable conditions and can be used to suppress weeds and stabilize dry slopes in deep shade.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination after natural cold stratification; or sow in early spring in a sunny, sandy seedbed and keep lightly moist. Plants bloom the second year from seed.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, well-drained, sandy or rocky, acidic soil in full sun; Maryland golden aster is a compact, drought-tolerant native with silky-hairy foliage and vivid golden-yellow daisy flowers from late summer through fall, making it among the best native alternatives to common goldenrod for dry, infertile sandy soil. In its natural habitat it occurs on dry pine barrens, sandy woodland margins, and roadsides throughout the coastal plain from New York to Florida and thrives in the heat of zones 8-9. The silver-hairy stems and foliage look attractive all season and provide an important late-season nectar source for specialist bees and monarchs on warm autumn days.

planting.note: Plant divisions or bare-root stock in spring in consistently wet to moist, acidic soil at the edge of a pond, stream, or bog garden; this species requires wet feet and will not thrive in well-drained conditions.

directions: Plant in wet to constantly moist, acidic, organic-rich soil in full sun to partial shade at pond edges, stream banks, and bog gardens; swamp candles produces slender candle-like racemes of small bright yellow star-shaped flowers in early to midsummer, lighting up wet shaded areas where little else will bloom. Unlike the invasive purple loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria), this species is a well-behaved native of eastern North America that stays in manageable patches without aggressive invasion. The plant often produces small plantlets at the leaf axils that drop and establish near the parent, gradually spreading into attractive colonies. An excellent choice for rain gardens, bioswales, and persistently wet spots that are too soggy for most perennials.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost when soil temperature is reliably above 65 degrees F; seeds germinate quickly in warm soil. Provide a strong trellis as the vine grows 30-50 feet in a season and fruits can weigh 5-20 lbs.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun with a very sturdy trellis; fluted pumpkin (ugu) is one of the most important leafy vegetables in West and Central Africa, particularly Nigeria, where the tender shoots and leaves are harvested continuously for soups, stews, and cooked greens rich in iron, zinc, and protein. The flesh of the huge ribbed fruits is also eaten, and the seeds are pressed for cooking oil. In the US it is grown as a perennial vine in zones 10-12 and as an annual in frost-free areas where the season is long enough. Harvest leaves and shoot tips regularly when vines are actively growing; the fresh leaves wilt quickly and are best used the same day they are harvested.
sources: FAO Plant Production and Protection Series
